mnemonics.title: ADApted GRADients
mnemonics.content: ADAgrad = ADAptive GRADient. Remembers: Adagrad adapts the learning rate by accumulating past squared gradients, making it great for sparse features.

mnemonics.title: RMS - Root Mean Square
mnemonics.content: RMSprop uses Root Mean Square of recent gradients to scale the learning rate. Think: 'RMS' = 'Reduce Mean Squared' - it controls how big the steps are based on recent gradient magnitudes.

mnemonics.title: Adam = Adaptive Moment
mnemonics.content: Adam = ADAptive + Momentum. It combines two ideas: 1st moment (momentum - like a ball rolling downhill) and 2nd moment (variance - adapts step size). The 'Adam' name comes from 'Adaptive Moment Estimation'.

mnemonics.title: Beta Values Song
mnemonics.content: Beta1 (0.9) for momentum, Beta2 (0.999) for variance - Remember: 'Momentum moves FAST (0.9), Variance is VERY precise (0.999)' - the order matters!

mnemonics.title: Adagrad Decay Problem
mnemonics.content: Adagrad 'collects' all past gradients causing learning rate to monotonically decrease. Mnemonic: 'ADA = Always Decreasing Activities' - the learning rate keeps shrinking, unlike others.

acronyms.usage: Use when training on sparse data (like text/NLP) where features appear infrequently. Not ideal for deep networks due to diminishing learning rate.
Use for online or non-stationary learning. Good for recurrent neural networks (RNNs). The 'root mean square' normalizes the gradient step.
Default choice for most deep learning problems. Combines benefits of momentum and adaptive learning rates. Works well with default hyperparameters.
Represents velocity/virection of gradients. Updated using beta1. Like a ball accumulating speed while rolling downhill.
Represents spread/magnitude of gradients. Updated using beta2. Controls step size like adjusting stride based on gradient intensity.
Added to prevent division by zero in Adam and RMSprop. Prevents numerical instability when gradients are very small.
Applied in Adam to correct initial bias in m_t and v_t estimates. Important in early training steps when t is small.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a backpack that gets heavier over time. Each gradient adds weight (squared gradient). As the backpack gets heavier, you take smaller steps (learning rate decreases). The denominator √(G + ε) keeps growing.

Think of a sliding window that looks at only recent gradients. Like a camera focusing on what's happening NOW, not the entire history. The exponential decay (γ, usually 0.9) controls how quickly old gradients fade from memory.

Picture two separate dashboards: 1) Speedometer (momentum) showing how fast you're going, 2) Brightness meter (variance) showing how intense recent changes are. Adam reads BOTH to decide step size. The bias correction is like calibrating both meters at the start.

Draw three mountains: Adagrad = steep cliff that flattens over time (shrinking steps), RMSprop = rolling hills with consistent pace, Adam = intelligent hiker adjusting stride based on terrain steepness AND momentum.

Vertical line with arrows: Each optimizer is a 'filter' that transforms raw gradient g(t) into update Δθ(t). Adagrad divides by accumulated squares, RMSprop divides by RMS of squares, Adam divides by corrected second moment estimate.

analogies.analogy: Adagrad is like a student who remembers EVERY book they've ever read. Initially, they explore widely (large steps), but as they accumulate more knowledge, they become more conservative and only pick 'safe' books (smaller steps). Eventually, they stop learning new things because they're too cautious.

RMSprop is like a weather system that only considers recent weather patterns to predict tomorrow's weather. It doesn't weigh historical data equally - recent storms matter more. This makes it adaptive to changing conditions (non-stationary data).

Adam is like a hiker with TWO traits: 1) Momentum (persistence) - once they start going downhill, they keep building speed, and 2) Caution - they adjust step size based on how rocky the recent terrain has been. They also 'correct' their initial uncertainty (bias correction). Most reliable for tough trails!

Compare to driving: Adagrad = gradually applying more brakes the longer you drive, eventually stopping. RMSprop = cruise control that adjusts speed based on recent traffic. Adam = GPS with both momentum (following the downhill path) and实时 traffic updates.

Imagine training on text data: most words appear rarely. Adagrad is like giving extra attention to rare words because they have high gradient magnitudes - it 'notices' what doesn't appear often. This is why Adagrad excels with sparse data.
